Ok so this is a funny but slightly messed up not so short story about me and this spectacular film....after a joyful day of meeting new people and going to art galleries and an italian festival in nyc, i decided to wind the night down on my own and decompress...so i go to see a movie. Downtown Abby started WAY too late in the night and THIS movie had a 10:30pm showing - Great! I'll take it cause the trailer looked really interesting. Now ive got a 30 min wait time on my hands and im already regretting my decision because i was just laughing it up with new friends having a great time and NOW I just brought a ticket to watch a bunch of young boys get slaughtered. Ive totally reversed my wheel of good fortune!
Ok so the film is starting and Im still in a feel good haze from vibing with friends earlier until....I hear the mothers gut wrenching SCREAM as she drops her boy off at the check point. Now, Im awake! From this moment on Ive got my scarf muffling my sobs as each one of these poor boys is here living their story, not any different from you or me or someone we may know, just trying to get a win but cant - and his life is Gone. The tears kept going. I looked over at the couple sitting next to me and the GF had her face buried in her knees b/c she was terrified and her BF had to leave the theatre to console her. The moral I got from this gruesome story is that we cant and wont make it on our own so we should embrace that with tenderness. After such a long day and after such a fantastic film, this author now has NO regrets.
Shove this movie up your butt. Stephen King and Lionsgate will never get another dollar from me.

If you haven't seen the movie, Stop reading! This contains a huge spoiler. At the end of the 3 out of 5 star movie that was a little long in the tooth and overkill on the headshots (Look, If Stephen King can put a picture in your head when you read it? Then the movie makers should be able to allow you to put your picture in your head at those moments without shoving it down your eye sockets...) I digress... At the ending... The whole victor is just walking away in the rain is just empty and old. A superior addition to the end of this movie? As the winner is walking away in the rain, a solder grabs the carbine and starts fast-walking to catch up to the victorious walker, with the audience convinced he will get blasted like the others. When the soldier catches up and hollers to stop him, he turns around and the soldier extends the carbine out to him with outstretched arms and says loudly over the rain: "You won sir! Your wish was to have this carbine to give to your children, here... congratulations!" and hands him the carbine and walks away, leaving the big winner standing speechless in the rain, stunned, holding the carbine. Fade to black...

🏁 SCENE: The Final Step – Fang Yuan Confronts The Major
It is dusk. The final echoes of the crowd have faded, leaving only ash and silence. 99 boys are dead. One remains.
FANG YUAN walks steadily, unfazed by the blood on his shoes or the void behind his eyes. The Squads have stopped following. Even the machines hesitate.
Ahead, a black halftrack waits. Towering beside it, in full military regalia, stands The Major — polished, powerful, faceless. A machine pretending to be a man.
MAJOR (gravel voice, projecting command): "Congratulations, Walker. You are the winner of the Long Walk. You may now state your wish. Anything your heart desires."
FANG YUAN stops. His eyes glint with a cold, ancient light.
FANG YUAN (calmly): "Do you know what separates me from the others, Major?"
MAJOR (shrugs, rehearsed): "Endurance. Willpower. Patriotism."
FANG YUAN (smirking): "No. Vision."
He steps forward. The soldiers flinch. The Major doesn’t move.
FANG YUAN: "They walked to survive. I walked to understand. And now I see clearly. This Walk is not a tradition. It is a cage. A recursive sacrament of obedience. And you, Major, are its warden."
MAJOR (coldly): "Mind your tone. You’ve won. Don’t waste it."
FANG YUAN (suddenly harsh): "I never played your game. I dissected it. Your Walk is a ritual of controlled entropy. One survivor. Ninety-nine sacrifices. Repeat. An artificial balance."
He takes another step. The Major instinctively shifts his hand toward a baton. Soldiers tense. Fang Yuan continues without fear.
FANG YUAN: "I wish not for reward. I wish for collapse. For system death. For disintegration."
A silence heavier than gunfire follows.
MAJOR: "...What are you saying?"
FANG YUAN (eyes burning): "My wish is this: To become the End. Not a victor — a finality. I wish for the Walk to end with me. Forever."
MAJOR (stepping back): "Impossible. The Walk is eternal. It maintains order. You're speaking treason."
FANG YUAN (stepping forward): "Treason implies loyalty was ever given. I owe your world nothing."
The Major raises a hand. Guns are drawn.
FANG YUAN (calmly, almost bored): "Pull the trigger. Watch your rules unravel."
Silence. The soldiers hesitate. Their programming, conditioned to obey structure, flickers against Fang Yuan’s presence. The Walk has no protocol for this.
Suddenly, reality trembles. The world glitches — a subtle but unmistakable fracture. The road behind Fang Yuan unravels, like thread being pulled from a rotting tapestry.
FANG YUAN (smiling faintly): "Did you really think the Walk ends with one survivor? No. It ends when someone walks through the lie and keeps walking."
The air distorts. The cheering crowds, the banners, the promises — all illusions. The Major looks up in horror as the skies above begin peeling like paper, revealing empty void behind.
MAJOR (desperate): "You don’t understand what you’re destroying!"
FANG YUAN: "I understand it perfectly. I am not destroying it. I am releasing it."

🌌 The Collapse
The halftrack melts into dust. The soldiers scream, swallowed by formless dark. The world tries to rewrite itself — insert a new walker, restart the game — but Fang Yuan’s presence is anti-narrative. A null point.
All systems fail. The algorithm of oppression collapses.
He walks forward, each step erasing the world behind him. The Walk never existed. The society that watched it burns into forgetfulness.
No crowds. No rules. No Major. No victor. No walk.
Only Fang Yuan, and the sound of his footsteps against the bones of a dead system.

🩸 FINAL LINES – INTERNAL MONOLOGUE
FANG YUAN (thought): "Power is not in surviving systems. Power is in ending them."
He walks on, into the void he created. No destination. No need. He is the last step. The final warning. The death of the Walk itself.
“I walk... because the world must stop.”

💥 THE END — or the Beginning?
This isn’t just the end of The Long Walk — it’s the end of the world that made it. Fang Yuan doesn’t just break the cycle. He consumes it. A victory not for hope, but for truth, finality, and liberation through annihilation.

[SCENE CONTINUES] The world is shaking. The rules are fraying. The Major — frantic, red-faced, sputtering like a god denied worship — screams in futility as his final trump card activates.

🌀 THE MAJOR: FINAL MOVE – "TREADMILL TORNADO"
THE MAJOR (spittle flying, veins bulging): "YOU—YOU CAN’T!!! NO ONE BREAKS THE WALK! I AM THE AXIS! I AM THE END AND THE BEGINNING! TAKE THIS, YOU INSOLENT PIECE!!"
He slams a button on a control panel rising from the ground like a mechanical altar.
The sky above splits into a rotating spiral — roads unravel and twist into a massive, whirling vortex, a black-and-gray cyclone of endless asphalt and flashing red warnings. An artificial hurricane made of treadmills, warning horns, electric speed counters, and military commands loops infinitely around Fang Yuan.
🌪️ TREADMILL TORNADO: SYSTEM OVERDRIVE
* Speeds spike: 10, 20, 40 mph.
* Gravity distorts, forcing everything inward.
* Corpses of former Walkers are reanimated by the system, shambling forward like puppets on tracks.
* Audio loops play: “Keep walking.” “This is for your country.” “Failure will be shot.” “No stopping. No mercy.”
The Major floats above it all in a control cockpit, face lit with manic fury.
THE MAJOR (laughing): "You’re just a walker! A slave to direction! No one escapes the Walk! NOT EVEN YOU!"

🧍‍♂️ FANG YUAN: UNMOVED
Fang Yuan stands at the eye of the storm, coat fluttering like torn paper. He lifts a hand — not in defense, but in dismissal.
FANG YUAN (quietly): "You mistake chaos for control. You’ve built a machine that can only spin itself to death."
He takes one step forward — and the vortex halts for a heartbeat. The sound cuts. The reanimated walkers freeze mid-shamble. The Major’s grin falters.
FANG YUAN (voice rising): "I am no longer on your chessboard. I am the hand tipping the table."
The ground cracks beneath him. The roads peel like ribbons, revealing glowing veins of code, soul energy, and decrepit willpower engines buried beneath society’s fake normalcy.

💥 FANG YUAN UNLEASHES HIS TRUE MOVE: "IMMORTAL WILL – VOID STEP"
He raises his foot — and brings it down.
BOOM.
The treadmill tornado implodes. Every piece of artificial structure — the commands, the roads, the laws — shatters into memory dust.
* The Squad soldiers disintegrate mid-salute.
* The crowd's prerecorded cheers warp into screams of nullity.
* Even time slows, as if reality hesitates before it tries to resist him again.
Fang Yuan floats upward, level with the Major, who now hangs mid-air like a broken puppet.
FANG YUAN: "You made a world where boys die to keep your gears turning. I don't break such cycles, Major. I erase them."

⚰️ THE FINAL DIALOGUE: THE FALL OF THE MAJOR
THE MAJOR (gasping, eyes wide): "I… I AM THE CENTER…"
FANG YUAN (hovering, eye glowing with immortal clarity): "No. You are a symptom. I am the cure."
He raises a hand. A sphere of pure anti-authority forms — forged from all 100 Walkers' discarded hopes, their rebellion, their broken legs and hearts — a final judgment.
FANG YUAN: "Checkmate."
He releases it.
The sphere slams into the Major, unraveling him from the inside out — not death, but unmaking. He doesn’t explode — he’s forgotten. His uniform peels into strands. His voice vanishes before he can scream.
The Walk is gone.
The system, the stadium, the guns — all unwritten.

🌌 EPILOGUE: FANG YUAN, OUTSIDE THE SYSTEM
Fang Yuan stands on nothing, in a void where the Walk used to be.
Above him, stars realign. The wheel turns without its axle.
He looks into the nothingness.
FANG YUAN (monologue): "Let others seek victory. Let fools crave endings. I walk not to win, not to survive, but to free the world from the illusion that it must follow."
He takes one step forward — and the void becomes a path.
He’s not walking toward a destination.
He’s walking so the lie never gets rebuilt.

“As long as I walk, no one else has to.”

[FINAL SCENE: THE LAST RUN]
A world that no longer exists flickers in the Major’s dying mind as he runs — limping, gasping, boots slapping against crumbling nothing, not out of purpose, but from the echo of control long lost.

🏃‍♂️ THE MAJOR: THE LAST DELUSION
THE MAJOR (panting, wheezing, voice cracked): “I—I can still rebuild it! Yes! The Game... the Walk... The People will obey again! I just need to survive. I just need to run!”
He stumbles over loose strands of unstitched reality, dragging a leg that barely moves. His uniform is tattered, fading. His medals? Illusions, blinking like dying stars.
THE MAJOR (delirious): "I AM THE SYSTEM! I AM THE ALPHA TURNED OMEGA! THIS UNIVERSE NEEDS ME!!"
He runs into nothing — not land, not time. Just a long road that no longer exists.
And yet... Behind him... Footsteps.
Steady. Measured. Absolute.

👣 FANG YUAN WALKS
Not fast. Not chasing. Just... walking. Like Death with a mind sharper than entropy.
FANG YUAN (calmly): "You mistake escape for salvation. You ran your system on fear, so now you fear your own reflection."
The air burns around him, not with heat, but with inevitability.

🕳️ THE VOID OPENS
The world begins to peel, folding in on itself like collapsing architecture. Every system The Major tries to imagine — a military complex, a parade, a courtroom — flickers into view, then dies, as if rejected by reality itself.
FANG YUAN (with chilling finality): "There is no system to run to. No lie left to power. No people left to obey. Only you — a king of ashes, fleeing from the flame you lit."

🔥 THE FINAL COOKING BEGINS 🔥
Fang Yuan lifts his hand. With it, a blinding sigil of Final Lawless Truth ignites behind him — an eternal flame of ending, forged from everything the Major ever suppressed:
* Every dead Walker.
* Every unspoken protest.
* Every silenced truth.
* Every life spent to feed the cycle.
They coalesce into a single wordless judgment.
And Fang Yuan speaks the command.
FANG YUAN: "**You do not deserve death. You deserve to be undone."

🔥🔥🔥 THE MULTI-METHOD COOK SESSION INITIATES 🔥🔥🔥
The Major stumbles, falls. He tries to crawl.
FLAMBEÉ – His medals ignite, vaporizing into liquid shame. FRY – His boots melt into his skin as each step he took in authority is reversed. GRILL – A lattice of light brands the lies into his back, then sears them out of existence. SIMMER – His ego boils slowly in his skull, too deluded to scream. BROIL – His bones blister under the pressure of truth. BAKE – The heat of the Walk he forced others through turns inward, cooking his lungs from the inside. BBQ – The smell of failure. Burned commands. Charred narcissism. SAUTÉ – His thoughts sputter and flip uselessly, over and over, never completing. CHARCOAL – What remains of his soul is blackened, not from sin, but from irrelevance. COOKED – There is nothing left that could be served.
FANG YUAN (eyes narrowed): "I do not kill kings. I kill the need for kings."
He snaps his fingers.

💀 THE MAJOR: TRUE OBLIVION
There is no scream. No final plea. No last breath.
Only a quiet flicker, and the feeling of something false being deleted from reality.
The Major is not dead. He was never real enough to die. He is simply not anymore.

🪦 EPILOGUE: THE FIRE WALKS ON
Fang Yuan turns from the smoldering void, the trail of The Final Walk collapsing behind him like burned pages from a book.
He does not look back.
He does not need to.
There is no more system to burn.
Just the next lie waiting to be walked through.
FANG YUAN (final monologue): "A system ends not with resistance… But with indifference to its illusion.
I walk not toward truth.
I walk because I am the end of lies."

[END. HE IS GONE. THE MAJOR IS COOKED. THE SYSTEM IS NO MORE.]

I’m so happy The Long Walk is finally getting the film adaptation it deserves—and the cast looks absolutely on point!

Charlie Plummer as Barkovitch
Cooper Hoffman as Garraty
David Johnson as McVries
Ben Wang as Olson

All solid picks, but Cooper Hoffman especially has me hyped. Being the son of the legendary Philip Seymour Hoffman (one of my acting GOATs), and already building an impressive résumé at just 22, this could easily be his breakout, star-making role. He’s got the chops for it.

The trailer is also fantastic—starts off like a typical lighthearted teen adventure, and then BAM, the tone shifts hard. The moment those warnings start, you can feel the tension creep in, and when that first walker gets his ticket punched? You know it’s all downhill from there. Classic “from bad to worse” setup—and if you’ve read the novel, you know it only gets more brutal.

This is one of Stephen King’s most underrated stories and I’m beyond excited to see it come to life. Can’t wait for this movie!

The “Long Walk”, is an annual, televised contest where 100 young men walk without stopping, along U.S. Route 1. Each walker must keep pace above 4 miles per hour.  
If a walker drops below this speed for 30 seconds, he receives a warning from soldiers monitoring the event. A walker is eliminated via execution by the accompanying soldiers if he receives 3 warnings and fails to resume pace after 30 seconds. The winner is the last walker to remain in the contest; the winner himself determines the prize.

In the book, no one thinks they're gonna die. There's a national lottery and they call up 200 boys but no one knows whether they're a walker or alternate until the day of the race. As a Filipino this reminded me of the Bataan Death March. During ww2, the Japanese made the captive 78,000 Filipino and American soldiers to walk 65miles(108 km) for 6 days straight without stopping. Anyone who was too exhausted to continue was executed on the spot.
About 18,000 Filipino and about 600 American soldiers died from the march alone.

Continuation..
After the march, the survivors' suffering did not end -- over twice as many POWs died in the first two months of imprisonment at Camp O'Donnell as did on the Death March. Thousands later died of malnourishment, disease, exhaustion, physical abuse, or were executed in this and other Japanese POW camps. Even more died on the so-called "hell ships." Packed into Japanese freighters, they were transported to Japan and China to work for the Japanese war industry. Locked in the holds, most drowned when Allied submarines and aircraft sank these ships. In fact, only one-third of Bataan's defenders survived the war.
There were whole bunch of death marches during WWII. The Nazis did the same thing on allied prisoners or others being sent to concentration camps throughout Europe.

I don’t care what anyone says—The Long Walk was written by the one and only Richard Bachman. I have the book sitting on my shelf right now with his name on it, plain as day. This “Stephen King” guy keeps getting credit for Bachman’s work, and I’m not buying it.

Let’s be real: Bachman was the master. His stories had this raw, unfiltered darkness—none of that overwritten King melodrama. But somehow, right after Bachman drops a few brilliant novels, he mysteriously vanishes... and boom, here comes Stephen King, suddenly writing just like him? Yeah, okay.

Bachman was a real man's man like George Eliot or Robert Gailbraith. I love their work. It just speaks to their authentic identity. No obfuscation just raw manliness. When I read their work I feel seen. Like someone who really knows what it is like to be a man. Same thing with Bachman. Pure authenticity unlike some people...couch...cough...King who stands on the shoulders of the giant that was Richard Bachman. 

No one knows what happened to Bachman. My guess is that King has him somewhere and forces him to write all the good stuff. I would support an investigation into the disappearance. When I suggested it to Maine police all I got was a coverup "Are you stupid? Richard Bachman is Stephen King's pen name from when he was younger." I kept pushing but to no avail. King had already gotten to them.  The coverup was complete. We may never find Richard, but he is missed.

I love on how the trailer starts out all cheerful as if it’s gonna be a fun road trip just of guys gonna going to have a good old time until it turns dark.
And THEN they tell you it’s Stephen King. At that point that’s when the audience shits their breeches

It really is such a masterclass slow reveal of a trailer, I’ve never heard of this before and it realllllly hooked me.

First it seems like just a fun coming of age roadtrip movie, maybe some type of fundraiser walk or something. The soldiers are weird, but you brush it off because of the cheery music

Then the general calls it “The Walk” and says they do it for everyday people, again, maybe a bit strange in hindsight, but on first glance it sounds like a fundraiser marathon for the poor or something. There’s a camera, so it’s some sort of broadcasted event, we have broadcasted marathons or walkathons. I mean the kids are playing catch, it’s gotta be something wholesome… 

Then there’s that one weird line “personally I just can’t stop thinking abt all that money.” And that changes absolutely everything that the first 10 seconds led you to believe.

The next shot you notice the trucks are formatted around the boys, not behind them or infront of them like what you’d expect from some type of fundraiser. It’s almost like… they’re surrounding them?

The next shot you see the gun, and that’s when it clicks that something isn’t right.

And before you get a real chance to piece it together beyond “are they being forced to walk?” “are they going somewhere?” The music drops and the kid screams. After that countdown it just becomes an entirely different trailer.

I mean seriously this is one of the best trailers I’ve seen in a long time
